data.title: Western Midstream: Pumping Out A Massive 9% Yield
data.description:
Article Thesis

Western Midstream Partners, LP (NYSE:WES) offers one of the highest dividend yields in the midstream industry. Its very strong balance sheet and the high demand for its pipeline capacity make it a lower-risk choice among high-yield investments, I believe. Western Midstream Partners has performed nicely so far this year, but shares nevertheless are not expensive. Overall, WES looks like an attractive pick for income investors who want an LP investment with a K-1.

Past Coverage

I have written about Western Midstream Partners once, in this article. With updated financials being presented since that article was published a little more than a quarter ago, and with Western Midstream reporting its upcoming earnings results soon, it is time for an update. For a broader introduction to the company, please feel free to read my previous article on Western Midstream Partners.

Peer Group Comparison

One can argue that the midstream industry, in general, is attractive to investors. After all, pipeline companies are not very dependent on commodity prices, making them resilient during recessions, while long-lived assets and fee-based contracts oftentimes result in highly predictable cash flows. While some midstream companies invest substantially in new assets, building out large and entirely new pipelines has become quite complicated due to regulatory concerns, which is why the existing pipes are oftentimes (nearly) irreplaceable, making them quite valuable. At the same time, with investment rates across the industry being lower than they were during the boom days, companies in the midstream space oftentimes generate strong free cash flows, allowing for deleveraging, strong income yields, and, in some cases, even a nice buyback pace.

But even when one believes that the industry, in general, is attractive, it makes sense to take a closer look at individual companies. Western Midstream has some advantages versus other midstream companies, as we will see:

Capital Efficiency

Western Midstream Partners' return on assets is highly compelling, at 13%, which is well above the other major midstream companies in North America -- Energy Transfer (ET), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Enbridge (ENB), ONEOK (OKE), Williams (WMB), and Kinder Morgan (KMI). Western Midstream's peers have an average return on assets of 5%, with Western Midstream's ROA being roughly two and a half times as high. In order to generate $1 of profit, Western Midstream needs to deploy less than half the money compared to its peer group average.

Western Midstream's assets are in high demand and see rising volumes, and its relationship with its main customer Occidental Petroleum (OXY) is very friendly -- OXY owns a large stake in Western Midstream. OXY thus has no problem in paying rates that are quite attractive for WES, as a large portion of Western Midstream's profits flow back to OXY via Western Midstream's dividends. These factors likely help explain why WES is able to generate returns on assets this high.

Balance Sheet

Western Midstream Partners does not only have strong returns on capital, however, as the company also has a fortress balance sheet -- at least compared to the other major midstream players. While it does not have a net cash position on its balance sheet as many big tech companies do, it has one of the lowest leverage ratios in its industry.

At the end of the most recent quarter, Western Midstream's net debt to EBITDA ratio stood at 3.0. Its peers have, on average, substantially higher leverage ratios:

We see that Enterprise Products Partners, another high-quality midstream choice, has a very appealing leverage ratio as well, at 3.2, which is just marginally worse compared to Western Midstream. The other midstream players employ considerably more leverage, however. While ET, ENB, OKE, WMB, and KMI all have solid cash flow profiles and are not at risk financially, Western Midstream seems like an even lower-risk choice due to its substantially stronger balance sheet. Western Midstream unsurprisingly has an investment-grade credit rating.

While some investors will prefer the business models of other midstream companies -- a case can be made for ENB's renewable energy exposure, for example, while EPD's products exposure is attractive as well -- it looks like Western Midstream is in an above-average position from both a capital returns and balance sheet perspective.

Looking at insider ownership, we see the following:

Insiders own 0.6% of Western Midstream, which is not overly much. Some peers, such as Energy Transfer, have way higher insider ownership. That being said, insider ownership at WES has been growing very reliably over the last couple of years, thus alignment between management and shareholders is improving, which is a good thing. As long as this trend remains in place, I do not see a reason to worry about the relatively low insider ownership percentage at all. In fact, the ongoing growth in insiders' exposure may suggest that insiders see Western Midstream as undervalued.

Earnings Recap And Outlook

Looking at the most recent earnings release, we see that Western Midstream showed attractive profitability, beating estimates easily. Cash flows looked strong as well, with more than $400 million in quarterly free cash generation, although some seasonal impacts made the Q2 number be higher than the average quarter impacted the Q2 number positively -- the forecast for the entire year is in the $1.2 billion range, thus Q3 and Q4 will be weaker again. That being said, $1.2 billion or so in free cash flow is still a pretty attractive result for a company that is valued at around $14.5 billion, especially when we consider that Western Midstream is not only investing in the maintenance of existing assets but also in new assets, which is why volumes keep growing at a nice pace. Free cash flow is forecasted to grow by around 19% this year, which is an extremely attractive growth rate considering the fact that the midstream industry, overall, is not growing very fast, and also accounting for the fact that WES is an income stock -- most income stocks are relatively stable, not fast-growing.

Looking forward, the company has guided for transportation volume growth of 10%-15% for crude oil and natural gas liquids in 2024, relative to 2023, while transportation volumes for natural gas and water are forecasted to grow by more than 15%, respectively. Most midstream companies grow at a slower pace, thus investors can be quite happy with the strong business growth Western Midstream is experiencing thanks to attractive assets and ongoing growth investments.

Dividend: High Yield And A K-1

Based on a quarterly payout of $0.875 per share, Western Midstream offers a dividend yield of 9.1% at current prices. This is more than one can get from Energy Transfer, Enterprise Products Partners, Enbridge, and so on right now. While none of these peers offer a low yield, their yields are less attractive than that of Western Midstream.

Importantly, Western Midstream issues a K-1. Whether one sees this as an advantage or disadvantage depends on one's personal circumstances -- some investors prefer K-1s as taxes are deferred, while others prefer to invest in companies without K-1s as K-1s result in some extra work. For international investors that aren't based in the US, K-1s can also be a hindrance. Investors that want to avoid K-1s may want to look among Western Midstream's peers without a K-1, such as Enbridge or Kinder Morgan, while others that like the deferred taxes may see this as an additional reason to own shares of Western Midstream.

Thanks to the company's compelling business growth, I expect that we will see further dividend increases in the coming years, although they would not necessarily be needed, considering the already pretty high starting yield.

On top of offering dividends, Western Midstream also returns some cash to its owners via buybacks, which is a nice extra. The company has reduced its share count by around 15% since 2020, which makes for a considerable boost to earnings and cash flow per share, and which makes the dividend safer over time. After all, WES needs to pay out fewer dollars on a company-wide basis when its dividend is kept stable and its share count shrinks.

Today, Western Midstream trades at around 12x free cash flow, while the earnings multiple stands at around 9. For a high-quality midstream company with above-average business growth, that does not seem excessive at all, I believe. The business is pretty insulated from commodity price swings, the strong balance sheet results in low financial risks, and due to its large position in WES, OXY does not have any interest in abusing Western Midstream, as that would hurt the value of its own investment. I would thus rate Western Midstream a low-risk pick. Western Midstream trades around 10% below recent highs, thus it has gotten more attractive again in recent weeks. Overall, I rate this high-yielding quality stock a "Buy" right here.

data.title: Elevance Health Stock: Looking Beyond Near-Term Issues, I'm A Buyer
data.description:
Elevance Health Overview

The writing was on the wall. Industry leader UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) reported fairly mixed Q3 2024 results on Tuesday, pointing to challenges in its Medicare and Medicaid units - no wonder shares of competitors like Elevance Health, Inc. (NYSE:ELV) were also dragged down a few percent by the results. However, yesterday's Q3 results from Elevance sent shares plummeting, and ELV stock even briefly traded at around $400 - a drop of more than 20%. Later in the day, shares stabilized at around $440, still a more than 10% intraday decline.

While I've covered managed care company The Cigna Group (CI) before here on Seeking Alpha, this is my first article on Elevance. I've always admired its solid earnings record, increasingly solid balance sheet quality (Baa2 rating with a positive outlook), good corporate governance, and especially its trusted BCBS (Blue Cross Blue Shield) franchise. Returns on capital are also solid and reliable, both in terms of net operating profit after tax (return on invested capital, ROIC) and free cash flow after stock-based compensation (cash return on invested capital, CROIC):

I also like the fact that Elevance has become an increasingly diversified company through acquisitions and internal developments. While the Health Benefits segment understandably contributes the lion's share of earnings, I like the diversification provided by the Pharmacy Benefit Management (PBM) business CarelonRX, which contributes almost 1/4 of the adjusted operating profit for 2023 (Figure 2). Despite its significant contribution, Carelon is among the smaller PBMs in the U.S., well behind OptumRx (owned by UNH), Caremark (owned by CVS Health Corporation (CVS) and Express Scripts (owned by CI), making it quite unlikely to be a primary target in the crosshairs of regulators (see this article on PBM-related risks).

Finally, ELV shares have been treading water since early 2022, so they have "grown" into their valuation. The stock wasn't expensive before yesterday's plunge, and even given the subdued near-term expectations, I would argue that a price-to-earnings ratio in the low teens isn't too demanding a valuation.

In this article, I will outline my views on Elevance's position in relation to potentially risky government plans and how I view the company's earnings prospects going forward. I will explain why I recently opened a position, which I expect to add to in the coming days.

Why I Am Adding To My ELV Stock Position Despite Recent Medicaid Woes

Elevance spooked investors with news that its Q3 2024 benefit expense ratio was 89.5%, up 2.7 percentage points year-over-year, largely due to a mismatch between budget cycles and rate adjustments. However, from a more distant perspective, the market is understandably concerned that managed care operators are coming under increasing government scrutiny and that their revenue growth could be impacted by austerity measures. Therefore, I think it is important to look at Elevance's exposure to government plans, such as Medicare Advantage and Medicaid.

If we disregard ELV's Carelon business and focus only on the Health Benefits segment, we can see that about one-third of the members were attributable to the government-related businesses at year-end 2023:

Elevance's exposure is clearly significant. The percentage of members vastly understates the exposure, and in terms of revenue, the Medicare, and Medicare businesses together contributed over 60% of the Healthcare Services segment's revenue (Figure 4), or 54% of consolidated revenue. This is not unexpected given the rapid growth of these two sub-segments, particularly in terms of revenue.

While the number of Medicare members increased by 2.8% on an annualized basis between 2021 and 2023, the number of Medicaid members remained virtually unchanged (a decrease of 0.5% on an annualized basis). Over the same period, revenue increased at an average annual growth rate of 15% and 13% respectively. Revenue growth slowed considerably in 2023, especially for Medicaid, and I think it's only reasonable to model slower growth rates going forward.

It is also important to remember that government-related businesses are significantly less profitable than the Commercial & Specialty business. Elevance has scale due to its large membership base, but pressures on revenue would certainly have a disproportionate impact on earnings - at least in the short term.

This warrants a closer look. Elevance has changed the reporting of segment profitability in its 2023 10-K and no longer provides figures for individual sub-segments. This is disappointing, but assigning the average profitability of prior years is a valid estimate in my opinion, especially given the stable segment profitability (Figure 5).

The review of margins and the estimate of segment profitability for 2023 are essential in that they provide an indication of the concentration of Elevance's profits. I have illustrated the contribution of both segments in Figure 6, which clearly shows the growing dependence on government business - not only in terms of members and revenue, but also in terms of operating profit.

The weak third quarter results have a significant impact on the full-year guidance, and normalization will most likely take longer than the end of the year. Management expects adjusted earnings per share of $33, down from "at least 37.2%" three months ago. However, what sounds like an inability for management to provide adequate guidance was, in my view, an entire industry being caught off guard. Elevance (formerly Anthem) management has met or exceeded the consensus for adjusted EPS in every single year since at least 2012.

Although not communicated directly, the benefit expense ratio is likely to be well above 90% in the fourth quarter, an approximate increase of 3.3 percentage points compared to the fourth quarter of 2023 (Figure 7).

However, despite the near-term challenges, management is still aiming for average annual adjusted EPS growth of at least 12% (slide 8, presentation Q3 2024). Revenue growth in the Health Benefits segment is expected to be in the mid to high single-digit range, while the operating margin is expected to increase to around 6% by 2027, which corresponds to an increase of 1.7 percentage points compared to 2022. Share buybacks and acquisitions will contribute to the rest.

But what if these expectations do not come to fruition?

The market, which sold off ELV shares sharply yesterday, clearly no longer believes in management's longer-term forecasts, and given recent results - including those of other companies - this is not an unreasonable expectation.

However, as this is an industry-wide phenomenon, I believe that the best-managed and well-capitalized managed care companies will overcome these issues. While Elevance cannot match the industry-leading profitability of its closest competitor, UnitedHealth, I contend that it is much more strongly positioned than other rivals such as CVS and CI (Table 1) and especially Centene Corporation (CNC) and Humana Inc. (HUM) (scroll down to "Profitability").

It is well possible that the profitability of these companies will suffer in the future, and with its significant exposure to government plans, Elevance will not be spared. However, I am confident that the best established, most profitable and most conservatively financed (Figure 8) companies will prevail, and the current headwinds will hopefully (and most likely) be used to improve efficiencies further. In my view, Elevance is one of the best companies in this sector, along with UnitedHealth.

In terms of valuation, it is of course reasonable to consider the somewhat weaker earnings outlook - even if management is sticking to its very positive longer-term view. The risk of government intervention is high, but I would argue that 2023-2024 is a period in which this aspect is being addressed fairly strongly for several reasons. These include persistently high deficit spending and likely structurally higher inflation going forward. Moreover, companies like UNH, CI, CVS, etc. are often stereotyped as adding zero or minimal value to the healthcare system. I would argue that skepticism of some business practices is warranted to a certain degree, so I believe the truth lies somewhere in the middle.

This skepticism is one of the reasons why I believe Elevance stock - despite the expectation of low double-digit earnings growth going forward - was already trading at a P/E ratio of about 14 before the crash. An implied PEG (price-to-earnings growth) ratio of just over 1 suggests that the market doesn't currently believe (and probably never believed) in these forecasts. Therefore, I would argue that the market's growing skepticism of managed care companies has led to a reasonable margin of safety.

Elevance has grown its free cash flow - as well as its earnings - at a low-teens rate recently and decades. As already noted, I don't think it's realistic to expect this trend to continue indefinitely. But even if we assume that Elevance suddenly slows to a low single-digit (!) free cash flow growth rate, the stock is not expensive. This is a very conservative scenario in my view, but given the high dependence on government plans and the controversial nature of the issue, I prefer to remain cautious.

Given Elevance's reliable free cash flow conversion, I believe it is appropriate to look at the stock's valuation through the lens of discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis. Figure 9 shows the results of the sensitivity analysis, which confirms the currently rather subdued expectations. A perpetual growth rate of only 1.5% at a discount rate of 7.6% (based on ELV's three-year beta of 0.59 and an equity risk premium of 6 %) is certainly not an ambitious expectation.

Conclusion

Elevance Health shocked the market with Q3 2024 results that were well below expectations. Full-year adjusted earnings expectations were revised significantly lower, and the 2.7 percentage point increase in the benefit expense ratio in particular was a major disappointment, and even more so was the implied continued acceleration in Q4 2024 (i.e., well above 90%).

While these near-term issues are certainly disappointing, I think investors should at least look beyond the gloomy outlook for the industry. Granted, the increasing reliance on Medicare and Medicaid makes a continuation of double-digit profit growth increasingly unlikely. Management will need to demonstrate considerable skill to offset the broadly expected significant regulatory headwinds. Persistently high deficit spending, most likely structurally higher inflation going forward, and the perception that companies in this sector add little to no value to the healthcare system make government intervention quite likely.

However, I firmly believe that operators with economies of scale, good diversification, a prudently managed balance sheet and industry-leading profitability in particular will overcome these challenges. The current environment is also likely to lead to an accelerated streamlining of profitability. So if - as unlikely as it may sound - the prospects for revenue growth over the coming years turn out better than the market currently expects, positive earnings surprises could be on the cards.

I don't expect ELV to be able to maintain its double-digit earnings (and free cash flow!) growth - despite management's positive assessment during the Q3 conference call. In fact, I never did, and neither did the broader market, given the long-term average P/E ratio of around 15. But even if we look at Elevance as a company that can only grow its free cash flow by 1% to 2% per year, ELV stock doesn't look expensive.

I recently bought a starting position in ELV stock, and at the current price of around $435, which translates to a free cash flow yield of 7%, I expect to buy more shares. The starting dividend yield of 1.5%, while being considerably higher than the five-year average of 1.1%, doesn't sound overly enticing, but ELV should be viewed as a total return investment. The company still has about $3 billion outstanding under its share buyback authorization, and an $8 billion increase was approved a few days ago. $11 billion of share repurchases would represent more than 10% of Elevance Health's current market capitalization.

However, due to the difficult outlook for the sector, I will limit the size of the position to around 0.75% of my portfolio value in terms of invested capital. This is even though Elevance is undoubtedly one of the highest quality companies in the sector. Although I think a drop to $350 is very unlikely - this would imply a P/E ratio of 10.6 - I will build my position slowly and remain cautious given the market's current nervous attitude towards managed care companies.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: Autoliv: Becoming Bullish On Margin Expansion And China Growth (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
My rating for Autoliv, Inc. (NYSE:ALV) stock is changed from a Hold previously to a Buy now. I have turned bullish on ALV, as Autoliv's latest Q3 disclosures draw attention to the company's growth prospects in China and its profitability improvement potential.

I evaluated ALV's Q2 2024 financial performance in the earlier July 22, 2024 update.

Look Beyond Mixed Third Quarter Results

ALV revealed the company's Q3 2024 results with its financial report issued on October 18.

Autoliv's top line decreased by -1.6% YoY from $2,596 million in the third quarter of the prior year to $2,555 million for the latest quarter. But its Q3 2024 sales still turned out to be +0.7% higher than the consensus projection of $2,537 million according to S&P Capital IQ data. This indicates that ALV's most recent quarterly top-line performance didn't disappoint the market.

Moreover, ALV actually outperformed the broader market by a wide margin. The company's organic sales growth declined modestly by -0.8% YoY in Q3 2024. In contrast, worldwide light vehicle production decreased by a wider -4.8% YoY during the same time period as S&P Global data cited in ALV's financial report. It is reasonable to say that Autoliv's Q3 2024 sales performance was good.

On the flip side, Autoliv's Q3 2024 bottom-line performance improved on a YoY basis, but the company's third quarter earnings were below expectations. ALV's non-GAAP adjusted EPS rose by +10.8% YoY from $1.66 for Q3 2023 to $1.84 in Q3 2024, and its actual Q3 2024 normalized EPS came in -7.5% lower than the sell-side analysts' consensus earnings forecast of $1.99 per share.

Adjusted operating margin for the company contracted by -10 basis points YoY from 9.4% in the third quarter of last year to 9.3% in the most recent quarter. ALV noted in its Q3 2024 earnings briefing that "lower sales and costs for supplier (legal) settlement" affected the company's profitability in the latest quarter. This could help to explain why Autoliv reported a lower operating margin on a YoY basis and its earnings missed expectations for the third quarter.

To sum things up, Autoliv's Q3 2024 results were mixed. ALV's Q3 2024 revenue was slightly above expectations despite a YoY contraction. On the other hand, the company achieved a positive low-teens percentage earnings growth for the recent quarter, but its bottom line was lower than the consensus estimate.

I think investors should look past ALV's Q3 performance and focus on Autoliv's future operating profitability improvement and its growth potential in China.

Autoliv's Operating Margin Is Expected To Expand In Q4 And Beyond

The company's operating profitability should get better in the quarters ahead, taking into account its FY 2024 management guidance and FY 2025 financial goals.

ALV left the company's existing full-year FY 2024 normalized operating margin guidance in the 9.5%-10.0% range unchanged, even though the company's actual 9M 2024 adjusted operating margin was much lower at 8.5%. This implies that Autoliv's Q4 2024 non-GAAP operating margin will have to be at the low-teens percentage level for the company's full-year operating margin to hit 9.5% based on my calculations.

Favorable Operating Profitability Drivers For ALV In Q4 2024

In the company's Q3 2024 earnings presentation slides, Autoliv mentioned that it is expecting a "significant Increase in profitability in Q4´24." ALV outlined the specific factors potentially contributing to operating margin expansion in its results' presentation, which are detailed in the chart presented above. Also, ALV indicated at its recent quarterly analyst briefing that its "costs for supplier (legal) settlement" for Q4 2024 will be lower on a QoQ basis, which is another positive for the company's fourth quarter profitability outlook.

Looking beyond 2024, ALV emphasized at its latest third quarter earnings call that "we remain fully committed to achieving our target of around 12% adjusted operating margin" for the intermediate term.

The Major Factors Supporting Autoliv's Mid-Term 12% Operating Margin Goal

As per the chart highlighted above, a decrease in the number of staff for ALV is one of the significant factors influencing the company's future operating profitability. In its most recent quarterly financial report, Autoliv shared that its "direct workforce" and "indirect workforce" declined by -5.9% YoY and -4.4% YoY in Q3 2024. This makes me more confident that ALV can improve its operating margin to 12% in the future, as the company has been making meaningful headway in shrinking its labor base.

In summary, ALV's normalized operating margin could possibly expand from 9.3% in Q3 2024 to the low-teens percentage in Q4 2024 and 12% for the medium term.

ALV Is Capitalizing On Growth Opportunities In China

There is the potential for Chinese OEMs to increase the safety content of their automotive models in line with global peers, and Autoliv has been growing its market share of domestic OEMs in China.

Autoliv highlighted at its latest third quarter analyst call that the "global OEMs have a higher average" safety content per vehicle than Chinese OEMs in general. ALV also noted that there is a "wider" disparity "between the premium and the low end" models in terms of safety content per vehicle vis-à-vis global OEMs.

It is realistic to think that the safety content per vehicle for Chinese OEMs' automobiles will most likely go up going forward. This could happen as local Chinese drivers become more conscious of the importance of safety features, and the Chinese OEMs expand in international markets where safety is already a key priority for consumers.

In its Q3 earnings presentation, ALV revealed that its market share for automotive safety solutions among Chinese OEMs had grown from slightly over 20% in 2021 to around 30% this year.

The Main Reasons For ALV's Market Share Gains For The Chinese Market In Recent Years

Autoliv has detailed the factors driving its market share up in the China market in its results presentation, which are highlighted in the chart above.

ALV already boasts a market share in excess of 50% for selected China automobile OEMs, as per the company's third quarter earnings briefing disclosures. The company has business relationships with over 60 domestic OEMs in China, so it is highly probable that ALV will continue to witness market share gains by either doing more business with existing OEMs or securing new clients.

In a nutshell, the company is growing its presence in the Chinese automotive market, where there is room for domestic OEMs to expand their safety content for their vehicle models. As a reference, China accounted for 19% of ALV's recent quarterly revenue, and there is the potential for the Chinese market's top-line contribution to increase for Autoliv in the future.

Variant View

There are certain risk factors which investors should pay attention to.

A key risk is that Autoliv fails to meet its FY 2024 margin guidance and midterm profitability goal due to unexpected cost increases or inflationary pressures.

The other key risk is that ALV's China business underperforms as a result of intense competition or a slow rate of increase in safety content for Chinese OEMs' vehicles

Conclusion

Autoliv is currently trading at 5.7 times consensus FY 2025 EV/EBITDA according to valuation data taken from S&P Capital IQ. Notably, ALV was valued by the market at as high as 8 times EV/EBITDA during FY 2010, which was the last time the company's operating margin exceeded 12%. I am rating ALV as a Buy now, as I view margin expansion and the China market's growth as potential valuation re-rating drivers, which could expand its EV/EBITDA to 8 times or better in time to come.

data.title: Docebo Improves Operating Leverage And Readies US Government Push (Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Outlook

Docebo Inc. (NASDAQ:DCBO) provides e-learning software and services to organizations worldwide.

I previously wrote about Docebo in May 2024 with a Hold outlook due to challenges in the lower end of its market segments.

Management continues to focus on broadening its customer base and moving upmarket in its efforts to reach larger, more profitable and lower-churn customers.

DCBO appears to be succeeding in its efforts and operating leverage is producing positive results, so my outlook is a Buy at around $46.00 per share.

Docebo's Market And Approach

According to a 2023 market research report by Grand View Research, the global market for e-learning products and services was an estimated $218 billion in 2023 and is expected to reach $851 billion by the end of 2030.

Reaching this milestone would represent an impressive CAGR of 18.6% from 2024 to 2030.

The primary drivers for this expected growth are cost efficiencies, flexibility in increasingly widespread hybrid work environments, and the need to upskill employees.

The pandemic has served to pull forward demand for these technologies, which will likely continue despite return-to-workplace policies among various companies.

The chart below shows the expected growth trajectory of the U.S. e-learning services market by type and through 2030:

A number of competitive or related e-learning industry players include:

  • Instructure

  • McGraw Hill

  • Absorb LMS

  • Coursera

  • SAP SuccessFactors Learning

  • Udemy

  • Saba Cloud

  • Udacity

  • Tovuti LMS

  • Cornerstone OnDemand

  • Captivate Prime

  • 360Learning

  • SumTotal Learning

It is interesting that a few public e-learning companies have been taken private recently.

Cornerstone OnDemand was acquired in 2021 in a $5.2 billion enterprise value transaction by Clearlake Capital, and Instructure has recently agreed to be acquired by KKR for $4.8 billion.

Apparently, these private equity firms believe the public markets are not properly valuing their targets, while they can add value to these acquisitions and refloat them or sell them in 2–3 years.

Docebo pursues prospective clients through various direct sales, partnerships & alliances, inbound marketing and acquisitions.

The pie chart below shows the breakdown of the firm's revenue sources by domestic versus international:

Recent Financial Trends

Total revenue by quarter (columns) has risen from enterprise and government segment growth, while operating income by quarter (red line) has moved higher in increasing operating leverage from internal efficiency improvements.

Gross profit margin by quarter (blue line) has remained relatively stable with no discernible trend. Selling and G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue by quarter (orange line) have decreased substantially due to the company's investment in automation initiatives. R&D expenses as a function of revenue (purple line) have trended higher, likely due to increased efforts in adding AI capabilities and enterprise upgrades.

Earnings per share (Diluted) have trended higher in recent quarters on increasing operating leverage and stronger topline demand ex-SMBs.

(All data in the above charts is GAAP.)

For balance sheet results, DCBO ended the quarter with $82.1 million in cash and equivalents and no debt.

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash flow was $27 million, and capital expenditures were $900,000.

The company paid $7.3 million in stock-based compensation in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was $19.7 million.

The net dollar decrease in outstanding shares for the previous four quarters was valued at a very high $151.9 million.

In the past 12 months, DCBO's stock price has risen by 14.2% vs. that of the benchmark iShares Expanded Technology-Software ETF's (IGV) gain of 31.8%, with a sharp drop associated with its May earnings report, as the chart indicates below.

Why I'm Bullish On Docebo

Docebo is seeing demand strength in its enterprise and government segments, some softness in SMBs and improved forward guidance.

Continuing with its increasing focus on moving upmarket to larger customers, the company has shifted to a value-based pricing structure by offering core bundles with add-on options.

This has resulted in reduced sales cycle length as it has simplified new customer decision-making processes.

As a result, average contract value has continued to grow, up 13% at the end of 1H 2024 versus 2023's results:

Management didn't provide a net revenue retention rate [NRR] for the most recent quarter, which is an important metric for determining the product-market fit and the company's efficiency at upselling and retaining customers.

In Q1 2024, management said its 2023 NRR was 104%, a relatively modest figure just above breakeven at 100%.

I suspect that DCBO's efforts to move upmarket may be negatively impacting its NRR results, so management is opting not to publish that information.

Management said its U.S. FedRAMP certification process remains on schedule for certification by the end of 2024 or early 2025.

FedRAMP certification is a program that ensures cloud-based services to the U.S. Federal government include standardized security, authorization, and monitoring capabilities.

The following graphic shows the frequency of various negative keywords and terms in the most recent management conference call with analysts.

The chart shows a significant reduction in negative word frequency compared to the previous quarter's sentiment indicator, a positive signal for the company.

data.title: Potential Opportunities In Pet Healthcare Stocks
data.description:
While you may think of healthcare as only covering human health, animal and pet health is also a growing sector. MoneyTalk’s Greg Bonnell discusses with Jared Ablass, VP, Portfolio Research, TD Asset Management.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell - When we talk about health care, you may immediately think of human health care products. But there is another subset of the pharmaceutical sector that could be worth a look-- pet care. Joining us now to discuss is Jared Ablass, VP of Portfolio Research for TD Asset Management. Great to have you back on the show.

Jared Ablass - Thanks for having me back on. I think pets, interesting topic. Who doesn't love a puppy, right? I think there's now 60% of Canadian households that own a pet.

So a lot of us are familiar with vet bills, and that money goes to somebody. I think the other reason that's interesting to talk about this is it's got a lot of hallmarks of what I would call a very quality industry. And I brought a couple of charts to just kick it off from the hop, a little game that I was kind of, in my head, calling "Animal Health or Human Health?"

Greg Bonnell - OK, so let's play the game a bit. Animal health or human health? Let's throw it up in the audience here. So what are we looking at?

Jared Ablass - Yeah, so we got three lines on the chart, the green, yellow, red. This is sales per share. What you've got is one animal health company, which is the largest animal health pharmaceutical in the world. That's Zoetis (ZTS), US-based. And the other two lines are the largest non-obesity human pharmaceutical companies in the world. That's Merck (MRK) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ).

So first one, sales per share, you've got green outpacing the yellow outpacing the red. It is an animal health segment. So if you guessed green is Zoetis, you'd be correct. Two things to notice, obviously, outgrowing over the last five years, sales per share, but also a more linear trend.

And if you flip to the next chart as well, I've got return on assets here-- same three companies, same three colors. You've got Zoetis in green, Merck in yellow, and Johnson & Johnson in red. Again, impressive for Zoetis. Out of animal health, you're generating the highest return on assets and the steadiest.

Greg Bonnell - All right, let's dig in a little bit deeper on this graph, this return on assets. Why are animal health companies managing to outstrip human health care companies in that space?

Jared Ablass - Yeah, it's a great question. It's interesting, specifically because Merck, the company that's in the yellow, they sell literally the largest drug in the world, full stop. It's a cancer drug, KEYTRUDA, next year expected to be $30 billion in sales from one product.

The obvious question is, how do you get more efficient with your assets than that-- $30 billion from one drug? And, really, I'll give you the answer, I guess, in three folds. It's a consolidated market.

Four players, top four players, have 65% of market share. What that means is there's very little patent risk in animal health. In human health, you've really got a treadmill where you work super hard to get an asset to market.

You sell it for as long as you can, usually 10 to 12 years. Then your patent expires. A generics manufacturer comes in, reverse engineers the pill or the shot, sells it for pennies on the dollar, and collapses the profit pool. That's what Merck, for example, will have with KEYTRUDA in 2028. They lose their patents there.

So it's a constant treadmill they're working. That's one thing. There's less of that patent risk in animal health. Second thing, it's a non-human, and, I guess, third thing as well, it's an out-of-pocket market.

What that means is there's no insurance pressures leaning on you for cutting prices and things like that. And out of nonhuman market, what that means is that the degree of red tape, we'll call it, is much less in the development process. If you throw up the next chart there, this is just showing drug development timelines-- human health on the top, animal health on the bottom.

A lot to read. I'll just walk you through it. You've got drug discovery on human health. It takes three to five years to discover a new molecule. That's the chemistry in the lab happening. You've got your preclinical testing. That's mice and rates.

You do the testing, you file, you get to phase I then you run phase II, phase III. Those are all human trials. And then, finally, you can file it and get it approved 12 to 15 years later. Animal health, much leaner process.

You get through it in 6 to 8 years. And not only is it the timeline that's different, but in human health, if you work through drug discovery, pre-clinical testing, filing, you get to phase I, on the chart, it looks like you're halfway through the battle. But actually, at that point, you're still only 10% likely to get that drug approved. So it's very rigorous and not to the same degree in animal health-- much easier process.

Greg Bonnell - So that's return on assets and some of the fundamentals there. You also showed higher sales growth than human health. What are the drivers there?

Jared Ablass - Yeah. Yeah, maybe I'll flip to the next chart here-- I brought a couple here as well. The first factor is really growth in pets. I've got a slide here, if we have it. Yeah, there it is.

On the left, you've got pet populations versus human populations. That's humans in orange, cats in green, dogs in blue. Since the year 2000, obviously, choppier data points on cats and dogs. That's just because it's owner-reported rather than a steady census number.

But, really, the driver of cats and dogs outgrowing humans in the US is the lifespan, which is on the right side. That's cats and dogs' lifespan increasing by 18%, 19% since 2000, versus humans about 4%-- that's like from 12 years to 14 years, roughly, for a cat or a dog.

So very interesting. I think there's a couple drivers to that. One of it is just it's a very under-medicalized area still. It's a $45 billion global market, which is less than 5% of human health-- even though it sounds large, less than 5% of human pharmaceuticals-- which means there's a lot of green space.

Zoetis is an innovation leader. They're talking about the next five, six years developing products for kidney disease, for heart disease, for cancer, which are all brand new markets for cats and dogs, but, obviously, very lucrative in humans. So that's kind of the white space that gives you upside runway. And then the other factor is there's the older generation, which probably think of pets just as that-- pets.

But now, half of pet owners are millennial and Gen Z, which much more tend to think of it as part of the family, willing to spend whatever it takes to keep them healthy. So all those factors combine to drive above human health growth rates.

Greg Bonnell - Very interesting trends there. Zoetis, obviously, factors large in the conversation when we're talking about pet health care. What does the rest of the landscape look like?

Jared Ablass - Yeah, it's very consolidated, as I mentioned. If you throw up the last slide there, it's just got market share on the right. Zoetis is the big chunk, 23%. Then you've got Merck at 19%. That is the same human health Merck-- it's actually a conglomerate, got an animal health business in there.

BI is Boehringer Ingelheim, again a conglomerate-- human health and animal health, privately held. And then Elanco (ELAN) at number 4, 13%, that is the other largest publicly traded player. I've got some of the pure play charts on the left side-- Zoetis, Elanco, and then a tail of others.

I think the story here is there's a couple that have lagged. There's a couple that are kind of in the middle. That's pharma-like performance. And then at the top, you've got Zoetis.

The reason it's been there and have been able to hold that position is it's an innovator. It's led the market-- I mentioned the cancer and things that might be coming. It just did that two years ago, created a brand new market with osteoarthritis pain medicines for cats and dogs. And before that, it had done the first triple parasiticide-- fleas, ticks, worms.

So a lot of innovation, and this is a market with real brand power since it's consolidated, which means, typically, your first mover advantage does pay off. There is a flip side to the coin. Zoetis spun out of Pfizer (PFE) in 2013. Elanco didn't spin out of Eli Lilly (LLY) until 2018.

So the longer you have on market, the more dedicated animal health research you can do and the more interesting drugs that are coming to market. So there is competition coming, but, generally, a market where leaders should remain leaders. And I think maybe just last thing, to put a bow on it-- if I think of my overall pharmaceutical coverage, I think you've got large cap pharmaceuticals like the Merck and Johnson & Johnson, which are typically very defensive-- in a hard landing scenario, they would be the ones that catch a bid.

Then you've got a group of biotech stocks, which are generally unprofitable, very torquey to rates. So that's that kind of segment. And then you've got the more secular, obviously, quarterly bumps along the way, but more secular stories. We've talked about obesity extensively, and I would also lump in the now hopefully more well understood animal health.

Greg Bonnell - So the animal health obviously has a lot working in its favor. What would they be the biggest risks for the space? You mentioned competition. What else could it be?

Jared Ablass - Yeah, competition is the big one, specifically for Zoetis. Elanco has a couple products challenging its key markets. The other one is just through COVID, we had a big puppy boom where a lot of people were adopting pets, staying at home.

It's been very choppy since then. There's been people surrendering their pets. They just didn't have the time for it anymore. And then vet visits, there's a lot of vets that burnt out through the pandemic.

So the vet visits have been down. There's just not enough capacity there. So those are the limiters on the story.

Original Post

data.title: WCBR: Underperformance Persists Despite High Cyberattack Risk
data.description:
Cybersecurity stocks were a hot trade from early 2023 to the start of this year. Things changed, however, after a weak report from Palo Alto Networks (PANW) and was amplified after a botched content update from CrowdStrike (CRWD) during the summer. Put it all together, and the WisdomTree Cybersecurity Fund (NASDAQ:WCBR) has sharply underperformed the S&P 500 since the middle of the first quarter.

I reiterate a hold rating on WCBR. I see the valuation has elevated while its technical situation is not particularly strong. Given the relative weakness, but decent seasonal trends at hand, I assert that staying on the sidelines is still the right play with this fund. Of course, sentiment could quickly shift bullish if we see a slew of cyberattacks.

Negative Alpha for Cybersecurity Stocks Since February

According to the issuer, WCBR is designed to track the performance of companies primarily involved in providing cyber security-oriented products that meet Index eligibility requirements. It can be used by investors to gain targeted exposure to companies driving innovation in cybersecurity technologies and can complement growth or technology holdings.

WCBR is a small ETF with just $107 million in assets under management, which is a small increase from $101 million when I last analyzed the fund in the first quarter. It’s not an overly expensive product given its 0.45% annual expense ratio, but there is no stated dividend. Share-price momentum has been decent lately, but stretch out the chart to a few years, and you’ll see that while the S&P 500 is notching all-time highs this month, WCBR is nowhere near its best marks.

Another concerning attribute is the ETF’s poor D- risk rating. That’s largely due to lofty standard deviation trends and somewhat high concentration. Finally, liquidity indicators are mixed – average daily volume is low at 24,000 shares, while the 30-day median bid/ask spread is high at 16 basis points. Thus, I encourage prospective investors to use limit orders during the trading day.

Looking closer at the portfolio, the 2-star, Bronze-rated ETF by Morningstar plots on the far-right side of the style box, indicating its growth bent. There is ample diversification in terms of size, but that also means there’s added cyclical risk, given the high 44% weight to small caps.

What catches my attention more is WCBR’s high 37.1 price-to-earnings ratio. Compared to its 11.0% long-term EPS growth rate, the PEG ratio is quite stretched.

WCBR: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

WCBR is a 100% Information Technology ETF. While no single position accounts for more than 6% of the fund, there are just 24 holdings, so there is material concentration risk.

We’ve seen this year that if one major cybersecurity name falters, it can mean bad things for the entire group of equities. And without a dividend, there’s little income cushion when volatility kicks up.

WCBR: A Concentrated Portfolio, No Yield

WCBR doesn’t have a long track record, so I analyzed seasonal performance trends of the Amplify Cybersecurity ETF (HACK).

The last 10 years of returns reveal that the October to November period has been strong, but December has been weaker. The first two months of the new year then often resume the bullish trend. So, in net, seasonality favors the cybersecurity bulls.

WCBR: Bullish Seasonality Through February

The Technical Take

With a high valuation, concentrated allocation, and sanguine seasonals, WCBR’s technicals don’t appear much better than when I last analyzed the product in February. Notice in the chart below that shares remain below not only the November 2021 all-time high above $30, but also the February 2021 peak around $21.

What’s encouraging is that the ETF is above both its long-term 200-day moving average and the shorter-term 50dma. But with a high amount of volume by price in the mid to high $20s, more churning of price action may be in the cards into year-end. That assertion is backed up by neutral RSI momentum trends, too. Support is seen at the year-to-date low of $22.

WCBR: Neutral Technical Trends, Low $30s Resistance, $29 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on WCBR. I see the cybersecurity ETF as being richly valued with bullish calendar trends, while the technical situation is mixed after peaking shortly before my previous analysis in the first quarter of this year.

data.title: Elevance Health: Why Invest Over UnitedHealth Group?
data.description:
UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (NYSE:UNH) and Elevance Health, Inc. (NYSE:ELV) are the two largest health insurers in the United States. While both play major roles in the industry, there are key differences in their business models and growth prospects that, for me, give Elevance an edge looking to the future. Despite UnitedHealth's stronger performance over much of the past decade, I believe Elevance's expansion into healthcare services, its strong position in Medicare and Medicaid, and its more attractive valuation make it the more compelling option moving forward.

Comparing Business Models: ELV and UNH

ELV operates under the Blue Cross Blue Shield (BCBS) brand in several states, focusing on both commercial (private) and government-sponsored plans.

Recently, the company has been expanding into healthcare services-a key part of the growth thesis I outline below. ELV thus divides its business into three main segments:

  1. Health Benefits - Currently accounts for around 86% of revenue and includes commercial insurance, Medicaid, and Medicare Advantage plans. While Medicaid membership has been declining due to eligibility redeterminations, ACA and employer-based plans are growing.
  2. CarelonRx - The pharmacy benefit management division mainly serves its insurance networks, but also caters to some external clients. Significant investment in infrastructure (e.g., delivery logistics and tech platforms) has driven double-digit revenue growth recently.
  3. Carelon Services - Focuses on healthcare services and is the company's fastest-growing division. External revenue growth (from non-ELV customers) jumped by approximately 50% in Q2 2024.

In simple terms, the business model centers on diversification, spreading revenue streams across the three segments. Doing so reduces financial risk (vs. being all in one) and supports long-term stability. Central to this strategy are the company's risk management practices and its deep local/regional relationships through the BCBS brand. By expanding beyond insurance into integrated healthcare, ELV aims to reduce costs and (presumably) improve outcomes, though this shift also introduces new risks given the complex and evolving healthcare landscape.

ELV and UNH both generate substantial revenue from health insurance, but they differ significantly in terms of diversification and scale. UnitedHealthcare (UNH's insurance arm) and ELV's Health Benefits segment both focus on commercial insurance, Medicaid, and Medicare Advantage. However, UNH's Optum division encompasses healthcare services, pharmacy benefits management, data analytics, and telemedicine platforms. This is divided into two segments for ELV (CarelonRx and Carelon Services) but operates on a much larger scale for UNH.

Moreover, UNH has a more global presence with operations in Latin America (notably in Brazil, Chile, and Colombia) while ELV operates domestically through its BCBS network. The integration between UNH's two segments is arguably more developed, while ELV is still building toward that level of integration. Overall, UNH is more diversified whereas ELV, at least for now, relies more heavily on its core insurance operations, with healthcare services playing a growing but smaller role.

Two other peers to note are Cigna (CI) and Humana (HUM), both smaller than ELV but sharing some similarities. CI has also expanded beyond commercial insurance into healthcare through its Evernorth division, which includes pharmacy management, behavioral health, and data analytics. This somewhat parallels ELV's Carelon Services, though CI's focus on large employer plans and other health systems is more specialized. HUM by contrast is more concentrated in Medicare Advantage while ELV has a more balanced portfolio, as described above.

Comparing Fundamentals: ELV and UNH

There's no debating that UNH has delivered better total returns for investors over the past decade, magnified by ELV's recent and steep decline:

However, when comparing growth and profitability metrics, the picture is more mixed. UNH has achieved superior and more consistent revenue growth, but ELV has outpaced its larger peer in EPS growth over the past decade. While UNH maintains a higher average operating margin since 2019, it has trended downward, whereas ELV's has modestly improved. In terms of net income, ELV has performed better, with significantly stronger growth than UNH.

However, ELV has a clear advantage when you factor in share buyback yield. Morningstar calculates this at 1.0% for UNH and 1.96% for ELV, resulting in total yields of approximately 2.5% and 3.5% respectively. For income-focused investors, this may be a meaningful difference.

The Bull Case for Elevance

With this as a backdrop, my investment thesis for ELV is built upon its long-term earnings growth, expansion into healthcare services, and an attractive valuation for investors versus its peers.

  1. Earnings Growth - ELV has achieved a nearly 15% CAGR in EPS over the past decade. This growth has come both from premium increases in its core insurance business and the rapid expansion of Carelon Services. Carelon, still in earlier stages compared to UNH's operations, offers significant long-term growth potential, especially with the recent acquisitions of Paragon Healthcare and Mosaic Health Platforms (both this year).
  2. Market Position and Tailwinds - ELV's strong presence in Medicare and Medicaid, coupled with its extensive reach under the BCBS brand, positions it to benefit from favorable demographic trends (read: America's aging population). The company is also well-positioned to capitalize on government healthcare spending.
  3. Valuation and Shareholder Returns - ELV trades at lower P/E and P/S multiples compared to UNH, presenting a potential value opportunity for investors. Additionally, the company's 14.5% dividend CAGR over the past decade, supported by a low payout ratio, demonstrates its commitment to growing shareholder distributions.

Valuation and Price Target

Management provided revised guidance for FY24, estimating full-year adjusted EPS of $33 with 2025 adjusted EPS growth projected to be "at least in the mid-single-digit percent range." Based on this, we might anticipate earnings growth as follows through FY26:

Based on these projections, ELV currently trades at 12.6x the FY25 estimate of $35.31 and 11.8x the FY26 estimate of $37.78. Both multiples are well below current valuations for UNH (20.5x and 18.7x) and HUM (16.5x and 13.3x). CI is the cheapest of the group, trading at 12.4x and 11.1x.

But what if ELV's earnings grow at half the projected rate? This would imply earnings of $34.16 in 2025 and $35.35 in 2026:
